title: Renal Cell Carcinoma: Diagnosis and Management
objectives: Recognize the classic and atypical presentations of RCC
Understand RCC subtypes and molecular features
Recognize paraneoplastic syndromes associated with RCC

sections.title: Epidemiology & Subtypes
sections.content:
**~4% of adult malignancies**

**Subtypes:**
• Clear cell (75%): VHL pathway, worst prognosis
• Papillary (15%): Type 1 and Type 2
• Chromophobe (5%): Best prognosis
• Collecting duct (<1%): Aggressive
• Oncocytoma: Benign, cannot distinguish on imaging

**Risk Factors:**
• Smoking (2x risk)
• Obesity, hypertension
• VHL syndrome (bilateral, young age)
• Acquired cystic kidney disease (dialysis)
• Family history

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Classic Triad (10%):**
• Hematuria
• Flank pain
• Palpable mass

**Reality: Most are incidental**
• 50% found incidentally on imaging
• Often asymptomatic until advanced

**Paraneoplastic Syndromes (20%):**
• Hypercalcemia (PTHrP)
• Erythrocytosis (EPO production)
• Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction without metastases)
• Hypertension (renin production)

**Varicocele:**
• Left-sided, doesn't decompress supine
• Suggests renal vein involvement

sections.title: Diagnosis
sections.content:
**Imaging:**
• CT with contrast: Enhancement = solid tumor
• MRI: For IVC thrombus evaluation
• Renal mass protocol

**Biopsy:**
• NOT required for classic imaging
• Consider for small masses, ablation candidates
• Metastatic workup if needed

**Staging Workup:**
• Chest CT
• Labs: CBC, CMP, LDH
• Bone scan if symptomatic/elevated ALP

sections.title: Staging
sections.content:
**T Stage:**
• T1a: ≤4cm, confined to kidney
• T1b: >4-7cm, confined to kidney
• T2a: >7-10cm, confined to kidney
• T2b: >10cm, confined to kidney
• T3a: Perinephric fat or renal vein
• T3b: IVC below diaphragm
• T3c: IVC above diaphragm
• T4: Beyond Gerota's fascia

**Prognosis:**
• Localized: 93% 5-year survival
• Regional: 70% 5-year survival
• Distant: 12% 5-year survival

sections.title: Management - Localized
sections.content:
**Partial Nephrectomy:**
• Standard for T1a (≤4cm)
• Preferred for T1b when feasible
• Preserves nephrons

**Radical Nephrectomy:**
• Larger tumors
• Unfavorable anatomy for partial
• Includes Gerota's fascia, ipsilateral adrenal (if involved)

**Active Surveillance:**
• Small renal masses in elderly/comorbid
• Growth rate monitoring

**Ablation:**
• Cryoablation or RFA
• T1a tumors in poor surgical candidates

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**Cytoreductive Nephrectomy:**
• Debulking in metastatic disease
• Selected patients only

**Systemic Therapy:**
• Immunotherapy: Nivolumab/ipilimumab (first-line)
• TKIs: Sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib
• mTOR inhibitors: Everolimus

**IVC Thrombus:**
• Surgical thrombectomy
• Level I-IV based on extent
• Cardiopulmonary bypass may be required

**Metastasectomy:**
• Consider for oligometastatic disease
• Solitary lung metastasis

keyTakeaways: Most RCCs are found incidentally on imaging
Classic triad (hematuria, flank pain, mass) present in only 10%
Clear cell is most common and has worst prognosis
Paraneoplastic syndromes occur in 20% of patients
Partial nephrectomy is preferred for T1 tumors when feasible
Immunotherapy has revolutionized metastatic RCC treatment

references.citation: AUA Guideline: Renal Mass and Localized RCC

reviewQuestions.question: A 55-year-old man has a 3cm enhancing renal mass found incidentally. He has a normal contralateral kidney and no metastases. What is the preferred surgical approach?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Partial nephrectomy is the standard of care for T1a tumors (≤4cm) when technically feasible. It provides equivalent oncologic outcomes while preserving renal function.
